title.english: The Healing Waters: Rongoā and the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
title.teReoMāori: Ngā Wai Rongoā: Te Rongoā me Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore traditional Māori healing practices (rongoā) through the lens of our local Mangakōtukutuku Stream, which has been used for generations by Ngāti Kahungunu for its healing properties. The lesson connects to pūrākau (traditional stories) of the taniwha who guarded these healing waters and the knowledge of local tohunga rongoā.
learningObjectives: Identify and name at least 5 native plants from the Mangakōtukutuku area used in rongoā using correct te reo Māori terms
Create simple instructions in te reo Māori for preparing a basic rongoā remedy using sentence structures learned in class
Explain the connection between at least 2 local native plants and their traditional healing properties using te reo Māori vocabulary
Participate in a mihi whakatau to acknowledge the mauri (life force) of the plants and waters used in rongoā traditions

activities.title: He Mihi ki te Awa (Greeting the Stream)
activities.description: Students visit the Mangakōtukutuku Stream bank to learn and recite a traditional mihi that acknowledges the mauri of the waters and surrounding plant life. They will learn specific vocabulary for local flora (e.g., kawakawa, mānuka, harakeke) and water features.

activities.title: Ngā Rākau Rongoā o Tātou Whenua (Our Medicinal Plants)
activities.description: Using plant identification cards with te reo Māori names, students work in pairs to match native plants with their traditional healing uses. They create simple te reo labels describing each plant's properties (e.g., 'He rongoā mō te mamae' - a medicine for pain).

activities.title: Te Mahi Rongoā (Medicine Making Practice)
activities.description: Guided by knowledge of traditional practices, students prepare a simple kawakawa tea or balm while using te reo Māori instructions and vocabulary for the process (e.g., 'tapatapahi' - to cut, 'tunu' - to boil, 'whakaranu' - to mix).

activities.title: He Pūrākau mō Te Awa (Story of Our Stream)
activities.description: Students learn and retell the local pūrākau of the taniwha who protected the healing waters of Mangakōtukutuku, using te reo Māori sentence frames and key vocabulary to share the story with classmates.

activities.title: Te Whare Rongoā (The Medicine House)
activities.description: Students create a collaborative digital or physical display showcasing their learning, including te reo Māori labels, descriptions of plant uses, and instructions for preparing simple rongoā remedies.

resources: Local kaumātua Hone Smith (Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge holder specializing in rongoā)
'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Te Papa Tongarewa online collection of rongoā artifacts (te papa.govt.nz)
Mangakōtukutuku Stream water quality monitoring kit and plant identification guides

tasks: Create and present a labeled diagram in te reo Māori of 3 medicinal plants found near Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Record a short audio explanation in te reo Māori describing one traditional healing practice connected to our local environment
Participate in a role-play demonstrating the proper process for gathering plants for rongoā while using appropriate te reo Māori phrases

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic te reo Māori sentence structures and vocabulary for specific purposes
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 4: Recognize and use tikanga associated with te reo Māori
Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organization - Level 4: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Science: Living World - Level 4: Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
